headline: MacIntyre's precision cuts through Miami's aerial assault
body: George MacIntyre did not need to out-yard Miami's offense to dismantle it. The Tennessee quarterback completed 19 of 24 passes for 270 yards and three touchdowns, posting an efficient 79 percent completion rate that stood in sharp contrast to Miami's Mertz, who connected on just 60 percent of his attempts despite throwing for 406 yards and four scores. Tennessee's defense bent but rarely broke when it mattered, holding Miami's explosive passing game in check during crucial possessions while forcing the turnover that shifted momentum late in the fourth quarter.

Miami's receiver Jackson accumulated 111 yards on seven receptions, a testament to the Hurricanes' commitment to the vertical game, yet that relentless aerial attack ultimately became a liability. Mertz was asked to do too much, dropping back 48 times while his running back Dixon managed just 10 carries for 67 yards. The imbalance left Miami vulnerable to Tennessee's situational defense, which tightened when the script became predictable. Meanwhile, Tennessee's balanced approach—131 rushing yards against 252 passing—kept Miami's pass rush honest and prevented the kind of sustained pressure that could have rattled MacIntyre.

The scoreboard tells only part of the story. Yes, Miami gained 533 total yards to Tennessee's 383, but yardage inflation without points is a losing proposition. Tennessee's offense scored when it touched the ball in the red zone; Miami's did not always convert opportunities into points. That conversion rate, born from MacIntyre's decision-making and Tennessee's willingness to establish the run early, proved decisive in a game where one offense prioritized efficiency while the other chased volume.
turningPoint: Tennessee's forced fumble in the fourth quarter, coupled with MacIntyre's touchdown drive immediately after, swung a tight contest into a two-possession game that Miami could no longer recover from.

unsungHero.name: Matthews
unsungHero.why: His five receptions for 93 yards and one touchdown provided the consistent intermediate threat that kept Miami's secondary honest and opened up the run game, yet MacIntyre's precision earned all the accolades.
